← Bioregional Organizations

🔵 Yukon River Inter-Tribal Watershed Council

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

An Indigenous-led grassroots coalition founded in 1997, uniting 70+ First Nations and Alaska Native Tribes across the Yukon River watershed. Guided by a vision to one day safely drink water directly from the river, YRITWC works through collaborative programs in water-quality monitoring, community education, stewardship, and sustainable resource governance. Their YRITWC Water Health Indicators complements scientific water quality monitoring with "cultural indicators", including elements like the taste of fish, the behavior of indicator wildlife species, and the quality of plants used for traditional medicines—dimensions of watershed health that conventional metrics might miss but reflect deep, multi-generational knowledge of the system.

Bioregional Context

The Yukon River—stretching over 3,100 km through Alaska, Yukon, and British Columbia—drains an expansive watershed of approximately 850,000 km². It holds profound ecological and cultural significance for dozens of Indigenous nations—Gwich’in, Koyukon, Yup’ik, Tlingit, among others—whose subsistence, social structures, and spiritual traditions are intertwined with its flows and salmon migrations. The watershed faces growing threats from climate change, permafrost thaw, mining runoff, pollution, and waste management challenges.

Place
Alaska Hwy, Yukon, YT, Canada
